coredata.dc:title: Wearable, disposable and non-enzymatic fluorescence nanosensor for monitoring sweat glucose through smartphone
coredata.dc:creator: Kansay, Vishal
Dutt Sharma, Varun
Srivastava, Vasu
Batra, Neera
Chakrabarti, S.
Bera, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: The promise of non-invasive health monitoring has fuelled the development of wearable biosensors for in situ detection of biological analytes in sweat. This paper presents a new fluorescent nanosensor probe based on boric acid functionalized and heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in paper-based analytical devices and integrated with a hydrophilic cotton thread-based microfluidic channel for the development of a flexible, wearable, biocompatible, and biodegradable sweat sensor for real-time measurement of sweat glucose concentration via smartphone readout. The morphological, physiochemical, compositional, crystallinity, and optical properties of nanosensors were extensively investigated. CQD particle sizes were found to be widely dispersed, ranging from 2.6 to 7.1 nm, with an average diameter of 4.38 nm. A dual response via fluorescence and colorimetric change was demonstrated for on-site sweat glucose monitoring using a smartphone camera to capture images in RGB (red, green, and blue) format and analyse them simultaneously by a customized android app, with quantitative results displayed on the smartphone. The limit of detection value for in situ measurement of sweat glucose concentration was 1.40 to 2.00 μM (25.25 to 36.13 μg/dL) and the limit of quantification value was 4.67 to 6.69 μM (84.16–120.44 μg/dL) in the pH range of 4.5 to 7.5. The developed sensor's performance metrics were validated using a standard clinical technique, which indicates strong correlation between two methods with Pearson correlation coefficient values of 0.83 and 0.80 for non-diabetic and type- II diabetes patients, respectively. Besides, interference, spike, and recovery studies were carried out with recovery rates ranging from 96.5 to 105.8 %, demonstrating good repeatability and reproducibility. Furthermore, feasible fluorescence quenching processes have been explored. In addition, the disposable and biodegradable nature of the nanosensor probe was studied by throwing it into the soil in the natural environment, where it dissolves almost spontaneously after three weeks.

head.ce:title: Wearable, disposable and non-enzymatic fluorescence nanosensor for monitoring sweat glucose through smartphone

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The promise of non-invasive health monitoring has fuelled the development of wearable biosensors for in situ detection of biological analytes in sweat. This paper presents a new fluorescent nanosensor probe based on boric acid functionalized and heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in paper-based analytical devices and integrated with a hydrophilic cotton thread-based microfluidic channel for the development of a flexible, wearable, biocompatible, and biodegradable sweat sensor for real-time measurement of sweat glucose concentration via smartphone readout. The morphological, physiochemical, compositional, crystallinity, and optical properties of nanosensors were extensively investigated. CQD particle sizes were found to be widely dispersed, ranging from 2.6 to 7.1 nm, with an average diameter of 4.38 nm. A dual response via fluorescence and colorimetric change was demonstrated for on-site sweat glucose monitoring using a smartphone camera to capture images in RGB (red, green, and blue) format and analyse them simultaneously by a customized android app, with quantitative results displayed on the smartphone. The limit of detection value for in situ measurement of sweat glucose concentration was 1.40 to 2.00 μM (25.25 to 36.13 μg/dL) and the limit of quantification value was 4.67 to 6.69 μM (84.16–120.44 μg/dL) in the pH range of 4.5 to 7.5. The developed sensor's performance metrics were validated using a standard clinical technique, which indicates strong correlation between two methods with Pearson correlation coefficient values of 0.83 and 0.80 for non-diabetic and type- II diabetes patients, respectively. Besides, interference, spike, and recovery studies were carried out with recovery rates ranging from 96.5 to 105.8 %, demonstrating good repeatability and reproducibility. Furthermore, feasible fluorescence quenching processes have been explored. In addition, the disposable and biodegradable nature of the nanosensor probe was studied by throwing it into the soil in the natural environment, where it dissolves almost spontaneously after three weeks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The potential for personalized health monitoring are being substantially expanded by the prolific research being done toward the development of wearable bioelectronic systems [1–4]. Wireless wearables sensors provide a non-invasive way to continuously transfer data to a user device and extract physiological characteristics indicative of health condition in real time. While blood has long been the gold standard for clinical diagnostics, non-invasive biomarker harvesting methods such as sweat, tears, interstitial fluid, and saliva have drawn the most attention as they are more convenient for daily health monitoring [5–7]. Additionally, subjects may not always comply with the pain of the invasive sampling process. Constant monitoring of these biomarker has the potential to complement laboratory-based blood tests in order to provide real-time monitoring of daily health status as well as early illness diagnosis and treatment.

Abnormal glucose levels in the human body can be hazardous to health. For this reason, developing glucose analysis techniques with excellent selectivity and repeatability is crucial for both clinical diagnosis and individual diabetic care. According to data from certain relevant studies, diabetes has become one of the most frequent chronic illnesses induced by modern lifestyles [8,9]. Relevant studies, particularly in the context of the global catastrophe, have shown that diabetes patients have an increased risk of COVID-19 poor prognosis because they have been in an abnormal metabolic state for a long time [10,11]. Hence, greater attention should be prescribed to diabetes population management by monitoring glucose levels in diabetic patients in real time.

There is a wide range of conventional methods for glucose measuring techniques, which are divided into three categories: invasive, minimally invasive, and non-invasive [12]. Enzymatic-amperometric and hexokinase are the primary techniques for monitoring blood glucose levels in labs [13]. Minimally invasive approaches need the extraction of some type of fluid from the body (e.g., tears, saliva, sweat, and interstitial fluid) in order to assess glucose concentration using an enzymatic technique. Invasive monitoring detects glucose levels in the blood directly. Non-invasive or minimally invasive procedures are used to acquire biological fluids containing glucose concentrations that correspond to those seen in blood. Because of this association, several alternative biofluids have emerged as promising analytes for the painless monitoring of glucose levels in the body.

Over the last few years, there has been a great deal of interest and effort placed into developing innovative sensors and improving the wearability of these platforms. Several glucose sensors have been described in the literature. Among the different sensing techniques established to date, those based on electrochemical and fluorescent signal transmission are prevalent [14–16]. The electrochemical sensors usually suffer from interferences from other electroactive species, a significant over potential, and/or stringent laboratory conditions such as strong alkaline solution [17–19]. Besides, certain electrochemical biosensors require additional reducing/oxidizing agents, such as dyes or enzymes, making these electrochemical devices more sophisticated [20–22]. In this regard, non-enzymatic fluorescence techniques, which fall under the category of non-invasive measurements, can help to avoid these issues.

Fluorescence probe performance is typically determined by the design of glucose-specific nanomaterials. In general, photoluminescent properties linked to glucose concentration can be obtained by sensing materials labelled with fluorophores or directly by sensor materials [23–25]. Among various luminous nanomaterials, zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials such as carbon quantum dots have attracted great attention owing to their tunable fluorescence emission, chemical inertness, biocompatibility, and non-toxic nature [26–28]. It has been observed that using doped carbon nanomaterials as sensing components can increase the sensitivity of fluorescence sensors [29–32]. Cotton thread, on the other hand, has been employed as a microfluidic channel in the manufacture of low-cost, low-volume microfluidic devices [33,34]. Nevertheless, as compared to laboratory-based testing, point-of-care or point-of-use allows for faster even more accurate results at a lower price. Another advantage is that it is portable, rapid, and simple to comprehend quantitative findings that can be read even by unskilled personal, which is even more useful in resource-limited places.

Hence, this study reports on the facile green synthesis of CQDs from Aegle marmelos fruit extract and its functionalization with boric acid for the development of a non-enzymatic fluorescence nanosensor for quantitative detection of in situ human sweat glucose noninvasively. Sterilized hydrophilic cotton thread as a microfluidic channel has also been combined with CQDs-based nanosensor assays on paper to develop a flexible, wearable, biocompatible, and biodegradable paper-based analytical devices for point-of-care diagnostics via smartphone readout. This nanosensor demonstrated dual response via fluorescence and colorimetric change for on-site monitoring of human sweat glucose using a smartphone camera to capture RGB (red, green, and blue) images, which were then analyzed by a customized Android app, with quantitative results displayed on the phone. A conventional reference methodology was utilized to evaluate the designed sensor's performance metrics, and the findings reveal a substantial relationship between the two ways (Pearson correlation coefficient values for type II diabetes patients were 0.80 and for non-diabetic patients were 0.83). Moreover, interference, spike, and recovery experiments were conducted, with recovery rates ranging from 96.5 to 105.8 %, indicating strong repeatability and reproducibility of the developed nanosensor. Furthermore, possible strategies for quenching fluoresce have been suggested. Additionally, the biodegradable and disposable properties of the nanosensor probe were examined by dissolving it almost entirely in three weeks in the soil in a natural environment.

Ripe bael (Aegle marmelos) fruit was collected as a precursor for CQDs from the nearby locality of the University campus (GPS coordinates: 30o25′ N, 77° 04′ E). In order to functionalize the natural carbon quantum dots, fresh lemon juice was used. Boric acid, glucose, sodium chloride, ammonium chloride, acetic acid, lactic acid were all purchased from SRL, India. Glucosidase test kit was purchased from Erba, Mannheim, India. The glassware was cleaned following standard protocol including treated with aqua regia solution. Using a Milli-Q system (18.2 MΩ.cm), double distilled deionized water was obtained and used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Aegle marmelos
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were created in a one-step hydrothermal method using Aegle marmelos fruit juice as a precursor. The synthesis of CQDs is described in the supplementary materials in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of boric acid functionalised carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The reflux method was used to functionalize as-synthesized CQDs with boric acid as a fluorescent nanosensor for non-invasive quantification of in situ human sweat glucose. For this purpose, 20 mL of aqueous CQDs solution was sonicated for 15 min with 5 mL of boric acid (0.5 M) solution before being put in a three-neck bottle flask for 5 h at 60 °C. The resulting solution was then cooled to room temperature before being centrifuged and filtered as described in the CQD synthesis procedure in the supplementary document.

For CQDs generated from Aegle marmelos fruit, the relative quantum yield values (ɸ) for quinine sulphate dye, employed as a reference standard material, were assessed.Our earlier publication contains the measurement information [35]. The obtained quantum yield was about 4.10 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of paper-based fluorescent glucose nanosensors
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-based fluorescent nano probe was also fabricated using biocompatible materials. The paper based fluorescent nanosensor was initially prepared by cutting the filter paper (Whatman) in ring shape of diameter 5 mm. The boric acid functionalized CQDs were examined at concentrations ranging from ∼ 0.05 to 0.2 mg/mL. The optimal concentration of ∼ 0.1 mg/mL was then combined with phenol red in a 9:1 volumetric ratio and sequentially dropped on paper before drying at room temperature for 20 min and repeated three times. The fabricated nanosensor probes were then kept in airtight sealed pouches at room temperature for future investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of wearable sweat patch, customized UV-chamber and android apps
ce:section.ce:para: A wearable sweat patch with three key components was designed: a hydrophilic cotton thread bandage, a fluorescent nanosensor probe, and a pH indicator. The hydrophilic cotton thread bandage utilized in sweat patches serves as an absorbent patch that makes direct contact with the skin, guiding sweat into the CQDs-based nanosensor probe region for the detection of sweat glucose. It is also coupled to a pH indicator and a nanosensor probe. During sweat-glucose measurement, a custom-built UV chamber as well as smartphone Android applications were employed. A UV chamber was custom-built to illuminate the nanosensor probes, which held an 11 W UVC tube (emission wavelength: 254 nm). A customized Android application, on the other hand, has been developed to snap images of the emitted lights from the fluorescent nanosensor probes using a smartphone camera. The custom Android application was developed to evaluate colour intensities and correlate them to quantify analytes for on-site image processing, based on the observation that the colour intensities of images processed by a smartphone's camera are typically proportionate to their red, green, and blue (RGB) values, while each base colour has a density range value from 0 to 255. In this study, a 108-megapixel camera on a Redmi Note 11 Pro (5G) smartphone was put as close to the UV chamber as possible (about 5–6 cm away) and used to capture all images at 10X zoom without the usage of flash. The RGB data from the captured photographs was then analysed and converted into quantitative data by the applications, and the results were displayed on the smartphone screen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and quantification procedure of glucose in artificial and human sweat
ce:section.ce:para: A standard stock solution of glucose was initially prepared at a concentration of 1 mM. Following suitable dilution of the standard stock solution with double distilled water, the various glucose concentrations were then prepared. The artificial sweat was prepared using the ISO 3160–2 standard formula, which comprises 20 g/L sodium chloride (NaCl), 17.5 g/L ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), 5 g/L acetic acid (CH3COOH), and 15 g/L lactic acid (CH3CHCOOH) [36]. Various glucose concentrations were then added into artificial sweat to replicate various artificial sweat samples. The following procedure was carried out in order to calibrate the sweat glucose measurement using a standard laboratory method. In this regard, four separate artificial sweat samples with pH values of 4.5, 5.5, 6.5, and 7.5 were prepared to simulate various human sweat conditions while adding different glucose amounts ranging from 1 mM to 1 μM. The produced artificial sweat was then combined with boric acid functionalized CQDs (concentration: ∼0.1 mg/mL) at a volumetric ratio of 1:2 in a 5 mL glass vial and subsequently incubated at room temperature for 5 to 10 min. The fluorescence spectra of each artificial sweat with known glucose concentration were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer from 290 to 750 nm at an excitation wavelength of 250 nm.

On the other hand, similar calibration with known glucose concentrations in artificial sweat was carried out with the developed paper-based fluorescent nanosensor sweat patch. The designed sweat patch may be conveniently applied to various parts of the body, such as the forearm, forehead, neck, and so on for healthcare monitoring. For this, a 2–5 μL sweat sample was dropped over the sweat patch and incubated at room temperature for 5–10 min. Following that, the samples were put in a custom-made UV chamber (11 W, 254 nm), where the colour shift of sweat samples with varying glucose concentrations was recorded using the smartphone camera in RGB format using the previously described customized Android applications.

Finally, the developed sweat patch was tested with human sweat samples to estimate the unknown glucose content, for which the wearable sweat patch was sewn on the body of four volunteers. Human sweats from two separate groups were studied: those without diabetes and those with diabetes mellitus (also known as type- II or non-insulin-dependent diabetic mellitus). Sweat secreted by the volunteer was collected through the sweat patch before and one hour after intake of clinically specified 40 g of glucose. The sweat patch was then put inside the UV-chamber, and the hitherto unknown glucose content of human sweat was determined using smartphone apps, as described beforehand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of performance metrics of sweat-glucose nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sweat nanosensor readings were validated further with a standard clinical procedure, especially a glucose test kit (Erba, Mannheim) based on the glucosidase enzyme, which is commonly used in medical laboratories. However, the clinical process for blood glucose level detection recipe cannot be employed as such because sweat glucose concentration is significantly lower than blood glucose concentration (the concentration of glucose in human sweat is typically between 0.06–0.2 mM, whereas it ranges between 3.3–17.3 mM in blood glucose) [37]. In order to recalibrate it against different known glucose concentrations, a freshly generated working reagent solution was mixed with an artificial sweat sample at a ratio of 1:5. After that, incubate at 37 °C for 10 to 20 min, or until noticeable colour changes occurred. Following that, UV–vis spectrophotometry was used to analyse the reaction solutions [38]. A calibration plot of absorbance versus artificial sweat glucose concentration was made and linearly fitted to obtain the absorbance per unit glucose concentration from the slope. Finally, real human sweat samples were evaluated using the calibrated results in order to compare those values with the developed nanosensor.

In every nanosensor investigations, experiments were repeated at least three-four times. Then statistical analysis was performed in terms of mean ± SD (standard deviation) with ANOVA using IBM®SPSS® Statistics Version 20.0.0 in order to calculate various analytes (glucose) concentrations for its own calibration curve [39]. The least significant difference (LSD) and Duncan's new multiple range tests were also employed after a one-way ANOVA at p value (probability value) < 0.05. MS Excel/Origin was used to perform a triplicate regression analysis on the kinetic data. In addition, the Pearson correlation coefficient (also known as the Pearson R test), a statistical formula often used to figure out the strength and direction of a linear correlation between two types of sensors, was also applied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodegradation study of the paper-based sweat patch nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The biodegradability was evaluated by disposing the probes consumed by the sweat nanosensor and the bioplastic nanocomposite nanosensor into the soil. Biodegradation is more likely in soil than in other settings such as water and air because soil is home to a diverse spectrum of microorganisms. The biodegradation conditions were regularly monitored until it was nearly degraded. The weight loss percentage of corn starch nanocomposite bioplastic was estimated with the following formulae:
biodegradibility
( % )
=
W i
-
W f
W i
× 100
, where Wi = initial and Wf = final weight of paper based sweat nanosensor [40].

The morphology of synthesized CQDs were studied by High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM). The HRTEM images were captured using a JEOL JEM 2100 plus with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The CQDs' composition and chemical analysis were further studied using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), with data obtained from a JEOL JEM-2100Fs. The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was performed using the Shimadzu IRSpirit, which has a resolution of 16 cm−1 and a scanning wavelength range of 500 to 4000 cm−1. The structure and crystallinity of the CQDs were studied using X-ray diffraction (D8 DISCOVER Bruker) with Ni-filtered CuKα
1 radiation. Scanning at 10 kV was implemented from 2θ = 10 to 80°. The Fluorescence and UV–visible spectroscopy of CQDs were acquired using a SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer. A 500 MHz Bruker Advance Neo NMR spectrometer was used for the 13C and 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy studies. The glucose concentrations were measured using customized Android applications with smartphone readout.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural, physicochemical and compositional studies
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal process parameters were optimized first before the morphological, structural, and physicochemical investigation of the boric acid functionalized CQDs since the major goal of this study is to take advantage of the fluorescence properties of synthesized CQDs. Firstly, the hydrothermal process's temperature and time were examined within the range of 110–200 °C for 45–720 min. The details are included in Figs. S2–S3 in the supplemental material. Figs. S4–S7 in the supplemental document provide insights on the investigation performed on how the hydrothermal process's temperature and duration influence the fluorescence properties. Finally, the optimal hydrothermal process condition of 110 °C for three hours was chosen based on the UV–Vis and fluorescence characteristics, as well as the consistency and repeatability of the process parameters.
The morphological characteristics of boric acid functionalized CQDs were subsequently examined by HRTEM, as shown in Fig. 1 (a)–(c).

The TEM micrographs reveal that the as-synthesized CQDs are tiny, but poorly distributed and agglomerated due to the absence of a passivation layer, and are quasi-circular or polygonal in shape. Meanwhile, the HRTEM image of the sample reveals the crystalline character of the CQDs as can be seen from Fig. 1(d). The lattice spacing was determined using the fast Fourier transform. The observed lattice spacing in the range of 2.1–2.5 Å is attributed to the (002) lattice plane of graphite [41,42]. Meanwhile, the statistical distribution of CQDs was calculated, and the corresponding histogram is shown in Fig. 1(e), revealing that CQD sizes are widely distributed between 2.6 and 7.1 nm, with an average diameter of 4.38 nm, suggesting that CQDs have a substantial quantum confinement effect. The distributions clearly have normal distribution features. We believe this is due to the nearly polygonal/spherical shape of the quantum dots, whose thickness is determined by the number of graphite layers. As a consequence, the diameter distribution overlaid on the thickness (number of graphite layers) distribution yields the TEM size distributions. This also adds to the variability, as the distribution is affected by the orientation of the CQDs on the TEM grid [43,44].

The elemental composition of CQDs, on the other hand, was studied using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), as shown in Fig. 1(f). The presence of high carbon (at. percent 75.4), oxygen (at. percent 14.4), and nitrogen (at. Percent 7.9) concentrations in energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy observations, as well as tracer amounts of boron (at. percent 1.05), potassium (at. percent 1.01) and calcium (at. percent 0.29) concentrations, implies that the as-synthesized functionalized CQDs. The composition of the core was further examined using NMR spectroscopy; the details are shown in the 13C NMR and 1H NMR spectra in Fig. S8(a)–(b). It has been observed that sp3 hybridized carbon atoms predominate over sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. Meanwhile, the crystallinity of the as-prepared CQDs was subsequently confirmed by XRD analysis, the detail of which is also described in Fig. S9.

As demonstrated in Fig. 2 (a)–(d), Aegle marmelos-derived functionalised carbon quantum dots include a wide range of functional groups and linkages. Stretching frequencies of 3178.3–3315.5, 2348.4, 2164.5, 1602.7–1646.2, 1392.1, 1184.5 and 1069.8 cm−1 strongly supported the presence of –OH, −O = C = O, −N = N = N, N-O, −B-O, −B-O-H, and −C-O-C respectively [45]. According to all of the investigations, hydroxyl, amine and carboxylic groups can be identified on the outer shell/surfaces of CQDs. Because of the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups, as-synthesised CQDs are extremely water soluble.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of paper-based sweat patch towards glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the study focused on the glucose selectivity and sensitivity of CQDs functionalized with boric acid. Fig. 3 displays the UV–vis absorption spectra of boric acid functionalized CQDs at pH values between 4.5 and 7.5 for a range of glucose concentrations between mM and µM. A distinguished immersion region between 486–603 nm by CQDs in presence of glucose are being substantiated when CQDs are dispersed in three different pH values (502–597 nm for pH: 4.5, 486–598 nm for pH: 5.5, 490–603 nm for pH: 6.5 and 488–595 nm for pH: 7.5) as can be from Fig. 3(a)–(d) [46–48].

Nonetheless, it can be seen that the absorbance steadily declines as the concentration of glucose lowers for all pH conditions. FL spectra, on the other hand, demonstrates concentration dependent selective quenching of fluorescence emission by glucose as shown in Fig. 4 (a)–(d) at pH: 4.5, pH: 5.5, pH: 6.5 and pH: 7.5, respectively [49–51]. The FL intensity was found to quench with increasing glucose concentrations. Further analysis details are provided in Fig. S10, Table S1 and S11. Notably, regardless of glucose content, FL spectra often get better when pH increases from its typical sweat pH value (∼4.5), as seen in Fig. S11(f).

Besides, understanding the interaction between CQDs and glucose is critical. Hence, Stern–Volmer quenching process was investigated through newly developed fluorescent paper-based sweat sensor for glucose. For this purpose, variations in green percentage were picked from the captured RGB photographs using customized apps through smartphone using the following equation [52]:

We believe that non-uniform glucose concentrations in diverse sweat samples, as well as non-uniform binding of CQDs' boric acid functional group with cis-1,2- or 1,3-diol units of glucose during repeated measurements, produced errors ranging from 2 to 10 %. The estimated
κ
SV
were found higher in neutral medium than acidic medium as shown in Table 1 , which is likely due to dipole interactions and adsorption of glucose in functionalized CQDs. Moreover, in order to assess the sensitivity of the newly developed paper-based sweat fluorescent sensor, the limit of quantitation (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD) have also been analyse via estimating 10 δ/s, and 3 δ/s, respectively, where δ is the standard deviation of fluorescence intensity variation, and s is the slope of the linear equation [53,54]. Both the LOD and LOQ of the fluorescent sensor show quite well pH-dependent limits of detection and quantification range between 1.40–2.00 µM (25.25–36.13 μg/dL) and 4.67–6.69 µM (84.16–120.44 μg/dL), respectively, as can be seen in Table 1.

A wearable, non-invasive sweat patch based on a non-enzymatic fluorescence nanosensor for quantitative sweat glucose detection was tested on humans as a proof of concept. The affordable and easy-to-use accessories for sweat glucose fluorescence measurement at point-of-care applications are shown in Fig. 6 .

The digital photographs of a protective cover for a commercial UVC tube (11 W, 254 nm) were constructed with a drawer for sample load and an eyehole to access the rear-camera of a smartphone, as illustrated in Fig. 6(a)–(d). Once the test sample is placed in the UV camera, a digital photo can be taken using custom Android apps in smartphone. The applications then analyse the photo in RGB format and display the concentration of glucose in the sample. Meanwhile, assembly of a cost-effective and flexible sweat glucose sensor patch is shown in Fig. 7 . It has also been demonstrated that how capillary force promotes the flow of sweat along cotton thread based microfluidic channels. On the other hand, the pH indicator monitors the pH level of the sweat, which usually varies between 4.5 and 7.5 depending on the various illness [55].
In addition, the designed sweat patch may be conveniently applied to various parts of the body, such as the forearm, forehead, neck, and so on for healthcare monitoring.

However, in terms of product designing, Fig. 8 presents a viable commercial packaging strategy for the newly developed nanosensor, which would improve their applicability for point-of-care applications in healthcare monitoring.

The sweat-sensor patch was sewn into a hydrophobic arm guard for on-site physical studies of human sweat glucose levels in order to further illustrate the viability of the devices for in situ human sweat analysis. Human sweats from two separate groups were studied: those without diabetes and those with diabetes mellitus (also known as type-II or non-insulin-dependent diabetic mellitus). Sweat secreted by the volunteer was collected using an absorbent patch via microfluidic channel based on cotton thread.

A smartphone was then used to analyse the collected sweat and determine its glucose levels quantitatively. The glucose concentrations in the volunteers' sweats range from 14.89 ± 0.86 μM to 15.94 ± 0.75 μM for non–diabetes volunteers, while it was in the range between 27.76 ± 0.89 μM to 49.75 ± 0.98 μM for diabetes volunteers, as can be seen from Fig. 9 (a). These values are in good agreement with the range of sweat glucose found in healthy humans and type-II diabetes patents. The volunteers then consume 40 g of glucose. They rested for one hour before exercising and having their sweat concentrations analysed once more. For every person, the sweat glucose levels are noticeably greater than the values obtained before to the glucose ingestion (see Fig. 9(a)). Likewise, a volunteer's blood glucose levels were assessed both before and after intake of the glucose. The glucose concentrations in the blood and sweat differ by two orders of magnitude, although their rising trajectories are in line with one another as can be clearly seen from Fig. 9(b). These findings imply that the increase in blood glucose caused by meal intake may also be seen in human sweating. However, a more general strategy that might collect sweat from people who are at rest or in a cold climate would be necessary to expand the uses of this sensor. Agonist medications (such as pilocarpine or lidocaine) have the unique capacity to cause human skin to produce sweat in specific areas, therefore using them may be an appropriate choice [36,56]. Table S2 provides an easy-to-compare summary of the glucose sensing performance of recent methodologies. The developed CQDs-based nanosensor has an easier and less expensive production procedure, improved skin affinity and moisture permeability, and a comparatively lower LOD. Further studies might be required in order to improve the sensor's long-term stability and reliability for its commercial uses in daily glucose monitoring.

The biosensor's key feature is its ability to detect interfering substances in addition to the analytes of interest. Developing anti-interference capability during sweat glucose detection is critical for the development of a fluorescence-based non-enzymatic glucose sensor. Interfering molecules, including ascorbic acid, uric acid, lactic acid, ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), sucrose, sodium and potassium chloride etc., typically coexist with glucose in sweat samples and can interfere with glucose detection [57].

Therefore, selectivity and sensitivity of those interfering molecules have been investigated further. The interference effect was investigated at least three times by mixing each interfering solution containing ascorbic acid, uric acid, lactic acid, acetic acid, NH4Cl, sodium or potassium chloride in proportions normally seen in human sweat with a specified amount of glucose (30 μM) [58]. Fig. 10 demonstrates selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching at low glucose concentrations (0.1 μM) compared to other interfering compounds, despite the fact that glucose levels in sweat samples are usually higher. Therefore, investigating the influence of interfering compounds demonstrates how effectively the constructed nanosensor distinguishes the target analyte molecule from the other compounds present in human sweat.

The performance metrics of the newly developed wearable sensor were evaluated through validation using a standard reference method, the specifics of which are described in section 2.8. Using both approaches, Pearson's correlation analysis was done to determine the levels of detected glucose concentration. The results of Fig. 11 (a) and (b) for those without diabetes and those with type−II diabetes, respectively, show that both approaches exhibit high correlation, with Pearson's r = 0.83 and 0.80 demonstrating good agreement between the two methodologies [57].

On the other hand, a sensor's robustness depends on its capacity to detect the target biomarker concentration while avoiding non-specific interactions. In order to make sure the sensor reflected the levels correctly, the spike and recovery response were assessed. The sensor was spiked with glucose concentrations ranging from 15 to 31 μM for both normal and type-II diabetes persons. The response was then examined using the developed nanosensor. With a recovery rate ranging from 96.5 to 105.8 %, the sensor demonstrated reasonable repeatability and reproducibility, as shown in Fig. 12 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQDs nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Despite the fact that a variety of fluorescence detection techniques have been developed until now that rely on the fluorescence quenching process, including the inner filter effect (IFE), surface energy transfer (SET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), electronic energy transfer (EET), and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), the actual process is rather intricate and interdependent [59]. As a matter of fact, photoluminescence of CQDs often corresponds to emissions related to the core and surface [60]. It is believed that the electronic transitions are caused by the recombination of electron-hole pairs at the localized π and π* levels of sp2 sites, which are located between the band gap of σ and σ* states of the sp3 matrix, are responsible for the emission in the visible range [61]. It has been proposed that the presence of electron donors or acceptors might effectively quenched the photoluminescence of CQDs [62].

Nonetheless, in the present investigation, the primary mechanism responsible for quenching the emission of boric acid functionalized CQDs following the addition of sweat-glucose may be attributed to the FRET between CQDs and glucose due to the fact that the emission spectrum of CQDs and the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of glucose have an integral overlap (see Fig. S12 for further detail).

In general, fluorescence quenching between donor and acceptor includes two fundamental mechanisms: (i) energy transfer via dipole–dipole interactions and (ii) electron transfer. We found the latter is more relevant. The covalent binding of the boric acid functional group with the cis-1,2- or 1,3-diol units of glucose is anticipated to promote FRET to glucose via electron rich fluorophore (CQDs), as illustrated in Fig. 13 (a) [63,64]. When exposed to UV light, photoexcited electrons from the valence band (VB) of a donor fluorophore (CQDs) may be injected into the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of an acceptor molecule (glucose), as seen in the electron transfer mechanism in Fig. 13(b). As the glucose concentration increases, the electron acceptance probabilities increase, and more electrons from CQDs are transferred to glucose due to increased FRET action, as shown by the increased overlapping area in Fig. S12. As a result, few electrons are left for fluorescence emission, and the peak intensity gradually decreases as evident from Fig. 4. Conversely, as was mentioned in the previous section, variations in pH have an impact on the photoluminescence properties of CQDs. As a result, we have also noticed changes in the pH-dependent FRET energy transfer efficiency, which was calculated to be 33.56 % at pH 4.5, 39.96 % at pH 5.5, 42.41 % at pH 6.5, and 48.65 % at pH 7.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodegradable study of the paper based sweat nanosensor probe
ce:section.ce:para: Degradation is an important phase in the breakdown of natural polymer substrate into environmental waste. Biodegradation in soil is usually caused by indigenous or transplanted/acclimated microorganisms, typically bacteria and fungus. Under aerobic circumstances, organic pollutants are transformed to carbon dioxide, water, and microbial cell mass. Composting is being utilized as an alternative option to landfilling or incineration for biodegradable materials. This technique converts biodegradable wastes into humic compounds, which are important as high-quality fertilizer for agricultural purposes.

Herein, the used nanosensor probes were dumped into the soil to evaluate its biodegradability in natural environment (30–32 °C, average humidity: ∼30 %). The biodegradation conditions were monitored on a regular basis until it was nearly degraded. Fig. 14 depicts digital photos of the degradation processes in chronological order, which declines around 85–90 % naturally within 20 days. Therefore, the newly developed Aegle marmelos-derived paper-bassed based fluorescent nanosensor for glucose detection in human sweat and quantification would contribute to the development of a disposable and ecologically product for point-of-care applications.

In conclusion, a non-enzymatic fluorescent nanosensor probe has been developed for the quantitative detection of glucose in human sweat. A one-step hydrothermal green synthesis method was utilized to create fluorescent carbon quantum dots from Aegle marmelos fruit extract. Boric acid functionalized CQDs were subjected to morphological, physiochemical, compositional, crystallinity, and functional group analyses using HRTEM, EDX, XPS, XRD, NMR, and other methods. It was found that CQDs doped with N, K, and Ca heteroatoms and had tuneable fluorescence properties that were sensitive to pH. It has been observed that the sizes of CQD particles range widely from 2.6 to 7.1 nm, with an average diameter of 4.38 nm. A dual response via fluorescence and colorimetric change was demonstrated for on-site sweat glucose monitoring. Images in RGB (red, green, and blue) format were captured using a smartphone camera, which was then used to analyze the images simultaneously using a customized Android app. The smartphone was then used to display quantitative results. In the pH range of 4.5 to 7.5, the limit of detection value for in situ measurement of sweat glucose concentration was 1.40 to 2.00 μM (25.25 to 36.13 μg/dL) and the limit of quantification value was 4.67 to 6.69 μM (84.16–120.44 μg/dL). The performance parameters of the developed sensor were validated using standard clinical method, indicating a significant correlation between both methods with Pearson correlation coefficient values of 0.83 and 0.80 for non-diabetic and type- II diabetes patients, respectively. Furthermore, interference, spike, and recovery experiments were conducted, with recovery rates ranging from 96.5 to 105.8 %, indicating strong repeatability and reproducibility. Also, potential fluoresce-quenching mechanisms have been proposed. Likewise, the disposable and biodegradable nature of the nanosensor probe was investigated by placing it in natural soil, where it destroys nearly spontaneously within three weeks. Despite the fact that this fluorescent nanoprobe can only be used once and that its fluorescence characteristics change depending on the synthesis conditions, however, it is evident that this nanosensor is very sensitive and selective for glucose. More importantly, this approach might be used to diagnose and monitor diabetes since glucose levels in sweat can contribute complementary information to blood glucose tests.

Vishal Kansay: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Varun Dutt Sharma: Formal analysis. Vasu Srivastava: Formal analysis. Neera Batra: Formal analysis. S. Chakrabarti: Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. M.K. Bera: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.
